title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.

Questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.

Limitations:
The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
This review assesses the submitted manuscript, which introduces an extension of PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) prediction sets into the domain of few-shot meta-learning. This framework operates under the assumption of an i.i.d. stream of tasks, where the objective is to rapidly adapt to a novel task using a minimal number of training examples. This adaptation is facilitated by prior exposure to a diverse collection of similar tasks, sampled independently from a shared task distribution. Establishing theoretically sound and non-trivial confidence sets for such few-shot scenarios presents a significant challenge. Building upon prior work, specifically Meta-CP [1], which leveraged task distributions to generate conformal prediction sets with marginal coverage guarantees across calibration and test tasks, this paper re-frames the problem within the PAC prediction sets paradigm. This alternative approach provides high-probability guarantees concerning the expected mis-coverage rate over new tasks, contingent on the specific observed calibration tasks. Conceptually, the relationship between PAC sets and Conformal Prediction (CP) sets mirrors that of Meta-PAC sets to Meta-CP sets, offering a distinct and often preferred set of performance assurances. The authors substantiate their algorithm's theoretical claims and practical utility through experiments on standard simulated few-shot meta-learning benchmarks (e.g., K-shot N-way image and relation classification) and a real-world heart attack prediction dataset, where "tasks" are defined by year and state.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

The manuscript is generally well-composed, and the experimental evaluation is compelling. A key strength, as effectively highlighted by the authors, lies in the distinct and advantageous guarantees offered by their PAC calibration framework compared to the Meta-CP approach. Specifically, Meta-PAC sets ensure validity on average across *all* subsequent tasks, rather than solely for the (n+1)-th task when marginalized over the full sequence of task draws. This characteristic is highly practical, as it aligns with scenarios where a consistent, fixed calibration set is repeatedly applied for predictions across numerous future tasks. Consequently, despite prior explorations of prediction sets within meta-learning contexts, this paper presents a significant and valuable contribution.

My primary reservation, beyond a few specific points detailed below, pertains to the clarity and accessibility of the presentation. While the authors manage a complex topic with numerous interacting elements reasonably well, the notational density can be quite taxing for the reader. The paper's comprehensibility could be substantially enhanced by introducing a high-level, step-by-step conceptual outline of the authors' objectives and methodology at the beginning of Section 4, prior to delving into the formal notation. For instance, an intuitive explanation of how Meta-PAC sets aim to identify a single, universal threshold that guarantees, with high probability, that the prediction set includes the correct label, considering averages over: (1) the random selection of calibration tasks used for threshold determination, (2) the random choice of task for threshold evaluation, and (3) the model's predictions on the new task using this threshold. While fragments of this explanation are scattered throughout the text, consolidating it plainly and intuitively upfront would be highly beneficial.

Beyond the general merits, several specific aspects stand out:
*   **Robust Theoretical Framework:** The theoretical foundation is compelling, representing a logical and valuable progression from the Meta-CP paradigm.
*   **Ingenious Calibration Approach:** The proposed calibration strategy appears both sound and elegantly designed. Initially, I harbored concerns regarding the computation of PAC-thresholds for individual calibration tasks, suspecting a potential oversight of a 1/N factor necessary for their simultaneous validity, particularly when subsequently applying the PAC method. This could lead to the final threshold being calibrated against a set of potentially invalid "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks. However, my subsequent understanding clarified that the crucial requirement is for the *output* of the PAC method to be i.i.d. (e.g., a random variable O valid with probability 1-alpha), rather than the individual outputs themselves being simultaneously valid. This insight allows the authors to efficiently bypass the 1/N factor, instead utilizing two 1/2 factors, which significantly improves efficiency.
*   **Convincing Empirical Performance:** The experimental findings are robust and supportive of the proposed method's efficacy.

Conversely, certain aspects warrant consideration as limitations:
*   **Static Threshold Limitation:** A notable point of concern is the reliance on a singular, unchanging threshold ($\tau$) for all subsequent tasks, irrespective of the size or characteristics of the adaptation set A. This contrasts with Meta-CP, which endeavors to derive a data-dependent threshold (via adjusted quantile prediction). Consequently, in situations with ample few-shot data or substantial inter-task variability, a dynamic, task-conditional threshold might offer superior performance. The current approach's fixed threshold means it doesn't adapt to whether a new task appears inherently difficult or straightforward.
*   **Practicality of Calibration Task Size:** The tightness of Clopper-Pearson bounds, particularly with small calibration task sizes, is a practical concern. The assumption of access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks appears somewhat idealistic and potentially unrealistic in many real-world applications, outside of highly specific contexts.
*   **Incremental Theoretical Advance:** While the theoretical underpinnings are undeniably sound, the work largely constitutes a direct synthesis of existing methodologies (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). As such, its overall impact is anticipated to be relatively modest, rather than groundbreaking.

## Questions

Specific clarifications would be helpful:
*   **Experimental Setup and i.i.d. Assumption:** In the experimental methodology, it appears that calibration tasks are drawn from 'N choose k' base calibration classes, while testing occurs on 'N choose k' base (disjoint) test classes. Could the authors clarify whether the samples drawn from the second set of classes maintain the i.i.d. assumption with respect to that specific set?
*   **Clarity on Lines 171-174:** The explanation provided in lines 171-174 is difficult to follow. My interpretation is that the objective is to estimate a value 't' such that $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. However, the role of 'g' remains ambiguous, and the introduction of a dummy label adds to the confusion. Further elucidation on this section would be greatly appreciated.

## Limitations

The authors have commendably addressed the inherent limitations of their work and considered its potential negative societal implications. It would be beneficial, however, to elaborate further on the concluding statement. Specifically, it is crucial to emphasize that this algorithm should not be perceived as a universal solution for few-shot confidence estimation. Its efficacy is predicated on several robust assumptions, and its guarantees primarily hold "on average" across new tasks. Nevertheless, despite these caveats, it undeniably represents a valuable advancement in the field.

## Rating

**Recommendation:** 6: Weak Accept.
The paper presents a technically sound contribution with potential for moderate to high impact. No significant concerns were identified regarding the evaluation methodology, resource utilization, reproducibility, or ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.
reviews.questions: Please refer to weaknesss above.
reviews.limitations: Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Here is the rewritten peer review:

## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This submission introduces a PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) prediction set algorithm tailored for meta-learning paradigms. The core objective is to enable the predictor to effectively adapt to novel, unseen tasks using only a limited number of labeled examples. Empirical evaluations across four distinct datasets showcase the efficacy of the proposed methodology, highlighting its PAC guarantee alongside the benefit of reduced prediction set sizes.

**Strengths:**
*   The manuscript exhibits strong clarity and is well-structured.
*   The methodology presented is logically sound and is bolstered by rigorous theoretical foundations.
*   The experimental validation is comprehensive, encompassing diverse domains and datasets for comparison.

**Points for Improvement / Weaknesses:**
*   A point of inquiry arises regarding the adaptation process: rather than merely reusing the meta prediction set parameters post-adaptation, exploring whether these parameters themselves could also undergo adaptation might yield further performance gains or theoretical insights.
*   The current approach appears to be predominantly applicable to metric-based meta-learning techniques, such as prototypical networks. Expanding its applicability to optimization-based methods, like Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML), would significantly broaden the method's scope and impact.
*   Regarding the experimental setup for the mini-ImageNet dataset, the evaluation is currently restricted to a 5-way classification problem. To provide a more thorough assessment of the method's robustness, it would be beneficial to include comparisons on more complex scenarios, such as 10-way or 20-way classification tasks.

**Questions:**
The specific questions for the authors are integrated within the 'Points for Improvement' section above.

**Limitations:**
The authors have adequately discussed the inherent limitations of their work and considered its potential broader societal implications, which is commendable.

**Overall Recommendation:**
**6: Weak Accept:** This paper presents a technically solid contribution with the potential for moderate-to-high impact. My assessment finds no significant issues concerning the evaluation methodology, resource requirements, reproducibility, or ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces and formalizes notions and algorithms for generating prediction sets with PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) guarantees within a meta-learning framework. The authors highlight that the proposed meta-PAC (mPAC) concept differs significantly from existing PAC notions designed for conventional machine learning settings (e.g., [3]) and from scenarios involving covariate shift (e.g., [6]). This distinction arises because the meta-learning environment provides adaptation datasets, allowing trained models to adjust to associated distributions without needing to estimate importance weights. The core contribution is the mPAC notion (Definition 3), which leverages an augmented calibration set sourced from $N$ distinct distributions ($p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$) to construct an $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a novel testing distribution $p_\theta$ with high probability $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is presented as a method to determine the prediction set's parameter $\tau$. This algorithm employs a two-stage approach: first, it establishes an ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold for each of the $N$ individual distributions. Subsequently, these $N$ derived thresholds are treated as a new sample to determine a final threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC assurance. Theorem 1 rigorously establishes the ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC guarantee for this proposed algorithm.

Strengths:
*   **Novelty and Conceptual Contribution:** The paper's primary strength lies in its innovative generalization of conformal prediction and PAC guarantees to the meta-learning paradigm. The introduction of the mPAC framework, specifically designed for scenarios characterized by multiple distributions and the availability of augmented calibration data, represents a substantial advancement over previous work. The proposed algorithmic solution effectively builds upon established PAC prediction techniques to address the unique challenges of meta-learning.
*   **Technical Rigor and Clarity:** The work demonstrates a strong technical foundation, with theoretical analyses presented in a commendably clear and precise manner. The arguments are sound, and the overall exposition is well-structured and easy to comprehend, which significantly facilitates the understanding of complex concepts.
*   **Relevance and Impact:** This research offers valuable theoretical insights into the application of conformal prediction within the rapidly evolving field of meta-learning. By providing a robust framework for quantifying uncertainty in meta-learning models, it addresses an increasingly important area for both theoretical understanding and practical deployment.

Areas for Further Consideration:
*   **Computational Aspects of Algorithm 2:** While the theoretical guarantees are clearly articulated, further discussion on the practical implementation of Algorithm 2, which relies on a grid search for the parameter $\tau$, would be beneficial. It would enhance the paper if the authors could provide more details regarding its computational cost and efficiency, potentially through analytical insights or empirical evaluation (e.g., exploring the impact of search granularity). Additionally, exploring potential strategies to accelerate this grid search process could further strengthen the paper's practical utility.

Limitations:
The authors have commendably addressed the inherent limitations of their work, as well as any potential negative societal implications, to a satisfactory extent.

Rating:
**6: Weak Accept**

This paper offers a technically solid and well-articulated contribution that significantly advances the application of PAC guarantees and conformal prediction to the meta-learning domain. The proposed mPAC framework and its accompanying algorithm are novel and theoretically sound, providing important insights into uncertainty quantification in meta-learning. While the work is strong, a more detailed exploration of the computational efficiency and potential optimizations for Algorithm 2's grid search would further enhance its practical applicability. Despite this minor point, the paper's overall quality, originality, and significance make it a valuable addition to the literature.

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
### Rewritten Peer Review

**Summary:**

Following the author's responses and revisions, which addressed most of my initial concerns regarding clarity and the attribution of prior work, I have elevated my assessment to a weak accept.

My final evaluation is as follows: I find the presented proofs to be sound and the discussion of related literature accurate. The paper notably offers the first instance of a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which holds intrinsic value. While this guarantee can be seen as a direct extension of existing theoretical frameworks, its specific application here appears novel and could be of interest to certain researchers. Furthermore, this work represents the second contribution to employ training-conditional conformal prediction within the meta-learning paradigm. It distinguishes itself from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. through its additional conditioning on the specific tasks, a feature that may prove beneficial to practitioners.

***

This paper introduces a distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee to the field of meta-learning and details an algorithm designed to achieve it. The proposed guarantee features a complex, triply-nested structure, where an inner expectation over a 0-1 loss is encapsulated within two further probabilities, conditioned on both the calibration set and the meta-learned parameters. The algorithmic approach essentially relies on two successive applications of binomial concentration. The authors also conduct extensive empirical validation across various meta-learning benchmarks spanning vision, language, and medical domains.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**

**Strengths:** I find the technical derivations to be robust and correct. The experimental section is thorough and comprehensive, providing ample evidence.

**Weaknesses:**

1.  **Complexity of Guarantee Interpretation:** The primary concern is the inherent complexity of the guarantee itself. Its multifaceted nature makes it exceedingly difficult to interpret, raising serious doubts about its practical utility. Users already struggle with the nuances of standard tolerance intervals, and introducing an additional layer of parameters significantly increases the cognitive burden, potentially hindering its adoption and effective configuration.

2.  **Challenges in Readability:** The manuscript suffers from dense and overly intricate writing, which significantly impedes comprehension. For instance, Figure 2 includes a substantial amount of extraneous information, lacks crucial color coding, and leaves several plot elements unexplained. The meaning behind the stripe orientations and the purpose of the box labeled "N" are unclear. Similarly, Section 4.1 is particularly challenging to follow, suggesting that its contents may only be accessible to a very specialized audience deeply familiar with PAC-type guarantees.

3.  **Limited Technical Novelty and Domain Contribution:** From a technical standpoint, the core result is not exceptionally groundbreaking, and the problem domain itself is not entirely novel, given the existence of previous meta-conformal prediction work that offers effective guarantees (albeit marginal on meta-learned parameters). The methodology of applying binomial concentration twice, rather than once, to further condition on these variables largely constitutes an extension of established results. Nevertheless, I do acknowledge the cleverness and ingenuity in chaining these concentration arguments twice, and the proposed algorithm is well-conceived.

4.  **Significant Attribution Issues:** There remains a substantial problem with the attribution of foundational ideas throughout the paper. The concept of training-conditional prediction sets was originally introduced by Vovk [21]. Rebranding this as "PAC prediction sets" and citing both [3] and [21] as co-sources is inaccurate. Vovk's work should receive sole credit for this foundational idea. Therefore, all instances of the attribution "PAC prediction sets [3]" must be removed. The same applies to the "PS-Binom algorithm," which is fundamentally identical to Vovk's Proposition 2b in [21]. While paper [3] can be credited for applying Vovk's work [21] within a deep learning context, [21] should be unequivocally cited as the sole originator of this concept, both in this manuscript and any future work. I also take issue with the rebranding of "training-conditional validity" as "PAC prediction sets," as these terms are equivalent and the former predates the latter. I would advocate for the rejection of this paper unless these attribution inaccuracies are thoroughly corrected.

**Questions:**

*   In practical, real-world scenarios, what constitutes "iid" tasks, especially outside of purely synthetic experimental setups?
*   Could the authors provide examples of realistic contexts where the proposed guarantee would genuinely hold?

**Limitations:**

I believe that the authors have adequately addressed the limitations. (Furthermore, I do not perceive any significant ethical concerns with this work that would impede its publication.)

**Rating:**

**6: Weak Accept:** This paper is technically solid and presents a moderate-to-high impact contribution. There are no major concerns regarding evaluation, resources, reproducibility, or ethical considerations, provided the critical attribution issues are fully resolved.

reviews.summary: The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.
reviews.questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.
reviews.limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

reviews.text:
## Rewritten Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces a novel methodology for constructing prediction sets during the meta-test phase, building upon estimated prediction sets derived from the meta-training adaptation and calibration stages. The entire framework is meticulously designed to align with the PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) learning setting, thereby offering a robust theoretical assurance for generating meta-test prediction sets without requiring further calibration. The proposed technique was empirically validated across four distinct datasets spanning three application domains, yielding competitive performance outcomes.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**

**Strengths:**
1.  The primary strength of this work lies in its strong theoretical foundation, underpinned by PAC bounds, which provide rigorous guarantees for the proposed method.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  The paper's overall presentation suffers from significant clarity issues. The notation employed throughout is frequently confusing and, at times, misleading, hindering comprehension.
2.  A broader conceptual overview is notably absent in key sections. For instance, Figure 1, which illustrates the process, fails to explicitly indicate that 'f' represents a pre-trained meta-model. This omission could potentially disorient readers familiar with meta-learning paradigms.

**Questions:**
1.  Regarding the evaluation metrics, both prediction set error and prediction set size are considered. While Meta-PS consistently demonstrates a relatively low set error, there are instances where its set size is larger compared to other methods. Given that the ideal scenario would involve minimizing both error and size, could the authors provide a more explicit and conclusive comparative analysis between Meta-CP and Meta-PS, clearly delineating their respective trade-offs and advantages?
2.  The authors have chosen PS-Binom for estimating the parameter \tau. Could they elaborate on whether the selection of different estimators for \tau would materially impact the method's performance?
3.  The score functions presented on lines 106 and 108 appear to operate over distinct input spaces. Could the authors clarify the reasoning behind this difference? My understanding is that 'f' functions as an off-the-shelf, meta-learned model.

**Limitations:**
1.  The paper is not entirely self-contained. Readers who are unfamiliar with the foundational work outlined in [1] will likely encounter considerable difficulty in fully grasping the presented concepts. I strongly recommend that the authors dedicate several paragraphs to provide essential background information from this referenced work.
2.  The evaluation of the proposed method's efficacy as a score function is limited to a single meta-model architecture, specifically the prototypical network. This narrow scope restricts the generalizability of the findings.
3.  The notational system, particularly concerning the score functions and the definition of prediction sets, remains a significant source of confusion throughout the manuscript.
    *   [1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

**Rating:**
**5: Borderline accept:** This paper presents a technically sound contribution where the strengths, particularly its theoretical guarantees, narrowly outweigh the identified weaknesses and limitations. While issues such as presentation clarity and the scope of empirical evaluation need addressing, the core methodology holds merit. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.

Questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.

Limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.original_rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Grounded Reinforcement Learning: Learning to Win the Game under Human Commands

reviews.summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.
reviews.questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).
reviews.limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.

reviews.text:
**Summary:**
This paper introduces a compelling new task designated as "Grounded Reinforcement Learning," which necessitates agents to optimize rewards while adhering to high-level language-based "constraints" provided by another agent or a human. The authors effectively demonstrate that conventional reinforcement learning (RL), constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) methods largely fail to perform adequately in the MiniRTS environment, which serves as a pertinent example of this problem setting, regardless of whether explicit commands are present or absent.

To address this specific challenge, the authors propose a novel approach named RED (Reinforced Demonstration Distillation). This method operates by iteratively alternating between pure RL updates and updates derived from behavior cloning. Through their experiments, the authors show that RED not only achieves superior performance but also demonstrates a stronger capacity for "grounded" policy execution. Furthermore, the paper includes comprehensive ablation studies that provide persuasive arguments in support of their proposed method.

**Strengths And Weaknesses:**

**Strengths:**
*   The paper presents a problem statement that, while perhaps building on existing concepts, offers a fresh and impactful direction for research.
*   The manuscript is exceptionally well-written and clearly articulated, making the methodology and results easy to comprehend.
*   The inclusion of human evaluation provides a particularly strong and convincing validation of the agent's performance and its ability to follow instructions.
*   This work holds significant implications for enhancing safety in AI systems, especially in applications requiring reliable human guidance and control.

**Weaknesses:**
*   My most significant critique pertains to the authors' conceptualization and use of the term "language." The current command representation relies on one-hot encoding, which is neither learned nor dynamically shaped by the agent. Crucially, it lacks standard components typically associated with natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax, or semantics. This fundamental mischaracterization of "language" somewhat undermines the broader claims of the approach.
*   The RED algorithm itself does not appear to introduce substantial algorithmic novelty, as it effectively combines two well-established learning paradigms (RL and BC).
*   The empirical results, while favorable, do not consistently show a dramatically significant improvement over the established baseline methods.

**Questions:**
1.  Could the authors clarify how their proposed approach fundamentally differs from a standard RL framework where the state space is simply expanded to include both command and game state information? Given that the command representation is not subject to a learning process, the distinction from a conventional augmented state-space RL problem remains somewhat unclear.
2.  Further justification is requested concerning the difficulty level of the opponent agent used in the experiments. Would demonstrating the method's advantages against a more challenging adversary provide a clearer and more robust validation of its capabilities?
3.  It is not sufficiently clear how the "command token" is meaningfully distinct from merely adding an extra dimension to the game state. Can the authors provide more detailed reasoning on whether this representation is learned, or if it possesses any characteristics typically associated with language, such as compositional structure, semantic meaning, or pragmatic properties?

**Limitations:**
Addressing the questions posed above would significantly enhance the paper's clarity and scientific rigor.
Additionally, the implementation of an actual language model for processing commands would represent a substantial step forward in truly demonstrating the effectiveness and generalizability of the approach within a more genuine linguistic context.

**Rating:**
**7: Accept**
This paper is technically solid, presenting a well-executed study with a clear methodology and thorough evaluation. It demonstrates high potential impact within the sub-area of human-AI collaboration and safety, or at least moderate-to-high impact across related domains. The evaluation, particularly the human studies, is commendable, and the work appears to be reproducible. No unaddressed ethical considerations are apparent. While there are legitimate concerns regarding the conceptual framing of "language" and the algorithmic novelty, the overall contribution, especially the problem formulation and the empirical demonstration of the RED method's effectiveness, warrants acceptance.

reviews.summary:
This work focuses on grounded reinforcement learning. Generally, it means finding a conditional RL policy that can perfectly response to high-level command. 

In this paper, the authors proposed a constraned RL algorithm RED to (1) train an agent that is capable of winning strategy games and (2) follow most human instructions. Concretely, RED uses RL to learn explorative policy while uses BC to imitate human demonstration.

Experiment results suggest RED achieves command-following policies and higher winning rate.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths

* The idea is concise. Use BC to learn human behavior while use RL to explore better solution.
* Visualization and videos provided are mostly clear and contain necessary information.
* Fig1 is illustrative and helpful to explain what is grounded reinforcement learning. (It would be better if Fig 1 has no background color and in PDF format)
* Code is released.

### Weaknesses

* This work assumes reward from interactive environment. (limitation)
* The code has no detailed guidance and documentation.
* Though I like Figure 2 and other experiments, the main experiment results in Table 1 is confusing and non-convincing.
* The idea is not novel. Conditional imitation learning is a long lasting topics. Refer to "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning"
* Many details are missing or confusing, such as Table 1 and Lagrangian method.
reviews.questions:
### Questions on method


Sec 4.3 says this work uses Lagrangian multiplier to solve the constrained optimization problem in Eq2. It turns out that, in my understanding, this is simply a weighted loss. A formal Lagrangian method should contain update process of the $\beta$, which is just ignored by this paper (or I miss, please point out). The later "iterative solution" also breaks the so called "Lagrangian method". 

---

Line 240 and Appendix A.2: Could you discuss more details on how you deal with action space? What does the "0/1 action for each unit" means? Who does the selection?

---

How to insert a NA command? IIUC, there exist a fixed set of possible commands right? So NA command should be just one more command in that set. You create embedding for each command in the set and than pass the embedding to the neural network.

---





### Questions on experiments

I can't understand table 1. IIUC, these four baselines have the policies that do not take command as input. Why they have different performance in Table 1 when applying different test commands? Please describe how you turn them as conditional policies. 

For example, since RL is trained without any objective to conduct distinguishable behaviors under different commands, why inserting different test commands will lead to different behavior?

---

I don't think Table 1 is convincing, especially when taking STD into account. 

Line 288: "Both RED and Joint policies substantially outperform Switch (BC) policy with sub-optimal commands (Random and Human Proxy), suggesting the conditioned behaviors are improved." This is a overclaimed statement. I can't draw this conclusion from Table 1.

---

I like Fig 2, which shows the dependency between commands and behaviors. Many "conditional RL" methods fail to prove that the conditional policy has casuality to the input command. This figure also shows that RED has much better "casuality" than RL.

My problem is that, in Figure 2, RL in 0% random commands achieves ~57% win rate. Does this value align with the RL performance with NA test command in Table 1?

Again, if RL is trained compelety without human command, then (1) where you can insert the command to the policy? and (2) why RL performance changes with the percentage of random commands?

---


Figure 6 says RL policy achieves similar win rate compared to RED. IIUC, RL policies are not conditioned on any human command. This result suggest that a human subject cooperates with RED trained conditional policy will lead to RL-comparable performance? This seems to be a violation to Table 1 "Oracle" result, which might lead to 

---

There is only one real gameplay footage provided (less than 2 minutes), with only 6 commands given, which is far less than convincing. Meanwhile, the commands seemed to be programmed/copy-pasted (I did not see any input lag), is there any real human intervention involved in recoding this video?

---

This game seems to be very complicated. Does your first intention of the application of this algorithm to be this high-level game? I understand the miniRTS environment is the only one that fitted the algorithm's need, but if you have alternatives (even if the performance is weak), would you also like to present them in the paper? Having diversity is a huge plus to your paper.








### Questions on paper




### Other questions or concerns



The [google drive link](https://drive.google.com/file/d/129ZV9CU6zUnp5B4v0p91Xaae8-M5zxHy/view) provided is **not anonymous**. This is a violation of the NeurIPS Code of Conduct (double-blind reviewing policy). Please action ASAP.

---

This paper has experiments involving 30 college students experiments, are attendees aware of their rights and responsibilities? A more explicit Ethics Statement should be included.

---

Conditional BC is not a new idea. Please refer to this work: End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning

---

Appendix D, as said in the checklist, has no information on human experiment. You moved them to Appendix E.

---

The website given in the appendix is very informative and I like the real-time gameplay footage, but there are several problems I want to point out:
- Code link is broken.
- Many grammar errors, will not be present here (plural or singular, tense, etc)
reviews.limitations:
The authors provide limitation section.

GRL is an interesting field. How to improve human-AI cooperation in test-time, how to learn a better human proxy, and how to further encourage, illustrate, quantify the causity between command and behaviors might be future directions.
